collection_title: Five Poems on the Ocean and the Origin of Life
curator: Prof. Edmund Halvorsen, Faculty of English, University of Oxford (fictional persona)

poems.title: The First Breath
poems.author: Prof. Edmund Halvorsen
poems.body:
Before the word, before the wing,
before the green of any growing thing,
there was the dark, the salted dark,
and in its hush, a single spark.

No lung, no name, no mortal eye—
only the slow, unhurried sky
of water folding into water,
mother, daughter, mother, daughter.

And from that fold, a molecule
learned the patience of the rule:
to copy, copy, copy, copy—
until the copy turned to me.

poems.title: Cradle of Salt
poems.author: Prof. Edmund Halvorsen
poems.body:
We call her ocean, but she is older
than the words we drape upon her shoulder.
She was the kettle, she the broth,
the lightning's anvil, the comet's froth.

In her tide pools, warm as breath,
she taught the carbon how to wed,
how to bind and how to break,
how to suffer for the suffering's sake.

Every tear you weep tonight
is a small return, a quiet rite—
the salt remembers, though you forget,
the womb you left, and the debt, the debt.

poems.title: Vent
poems.author: Prof. Edmund Halvorsen
poems.body:
Down where the sun has never been,
where pressure crushes light to stone,
a chimney smokes its mineral hymn
and life begins, and lives alone.

No chlorophyll, no leaf, no bloom—
just sulfur singing in the gloom,
and tube-worms swaying, blind and bright,
their crimson plumes a kind of light.

We thought the sun was all our cause.
We were mistaken. There are laws
that older, deeper currents keep—
and life climbs upward from the deep.

poems.title: Letter to My Ancestor
poems.author: Prof. Edmund Halvorsen
poems.body:
Dear small thing, dear shimmer, dear
translucent comma in the early sea—
I write to thank you, four billion years
too late, for choosing to be.

You could have stayed a slick of oil,
an accident the tide dissolved.
Instead you doubled, learned to spoil,
to hunger, hurry, be involved.

I carry you in every cell,
in every salt-bright human tear.
Forgive the noise, the steel, the smell.
Forgive the world you bore. I'm here.

poems.title: What the Tide Said
poems.author: Prof. Edmund Halvorsen
poems.body:
I made you, said the tide,
though you have forgotten my face.
I rocked your first division,
I was your first embrace.

You walked away on borrowed bone,
you learned to breathe the thinner air,
you built your cities out of stone
and called the distance everywhere.

But come back, come back at evening,
lay your ear against the shore—
that sound is not the sea's complaint.
It is the sound of something more:

the long, slow welcome of the deep,
the lullaby it cannot keep
from singing, even now, to you—
its child, its strange, its overdue.
